sections.title: D Implementation Details
sections.content: D.1 RL Details Preliminary of DiffusionNFT. Unlike traditional reinforcement learning (RL) methods that optimize generative models through policy-gradient objectives [53, 82], Diffusion Negative-aware Finetuning (DiffusionNFT) [24] performs reward-based policy optimization directly within the forward diffusion process using the flow-matching objective. Its key idea is to use the reward signal to distinguish desirable and undesirable generation directions. Specifically, DiffusionNFT trains the flow-matching model (FMM) to learn not only positive velocity v+(xt, c, t) that moves samples toward high-reward generations, but also negative velocity v(xt, c, t) that represents directions the model should avoid. The core policy-optimization loss is defined as: L(θ) = c,πold(x0c),t v+ θ (xt, c, t) v2 2 + (1 r) θ (xt, c, t) v2 2 , where denotes the target velocity field, and [0, 1] is the normalized reward interpreted as the optimality probability of the generated sample. The positive and negative velocities, v+ and θ , are implicitly defined as linear combinations of the old policy vold and the current policy vθ, θ controlled by weighting coefficient β: v+ θ θ (xt, c, t) := (1 β) vold(xt, c, t) + β vθ(xt, c, t), (xt, c, t) := (1 + β) vold(xt, c, t) β vθ(xt, c, t). Intuitively, high-reward samples assign larger weight to the positive velocity term, encouraging 33 Figure 17 Each row shows densely captioned image-text pair from our Lens-800M training set. the current policy to move toward desirable directions. Conversely, low-reward samples emphasize the negative velocity term, pushing the model away from undesirable generation trajectories. Since unconstrained raw rewards rraw(x0, c) may vary substantially in scale and distribution across prompts, DiffusionNFT converts them into bounded optimality probability [0, 1]: r(x0, c) := 1 2 + 1 clip " rraw(x0, c) πold(c) Zc (cid:2)rraw(x0, c)(cid:3) # , 1, , where Zc > 0 is normalization factor, typically set to the global standard deviation of rewards. 34 This normalization stabilizes training by mapping raw reward differences into bounded probabilitylike signal, enabling the model to balance positive-direction learning and negative-direction avoidance during finetuning. Optimization Details. Starting from the pre-trained checkpoint, we perform reinforcement learning (RL) for 180 steps on the Lens-RL-8K dataset using 64 NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. To maintain high-fidelity generation across diverse image layouts, we use mixed-resolution training buckets with fixed base area of 10242. Specifically, we consider nine aspect ratios: 736 1472, 768 1376, 832 1248, 864 1152, 1024 1024, 1152 864, 1248 832, 1376 768, and 1472 736. We fine-tune the diffusion Transformer using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) [83], with rank r=64 and scaling factor α=128. For the RL objective, we follow DiffusionNFT [24] and adopt group-based optimization strategy, using 48 groups per epoch and group size of 24. For each clean image in the dataset, we apply forward noising and compute the training loss at the corresponding sampling timesteps. We use second-order ODE sampler for trajectory collection and incorporate adaptive time weighting to stabilize optimization. To reduce reward hacking and preserve generation diversity, we apply KL-divergence penalty with coefficient βKL=1 104. We optimize the model using AdamW with β1=0.9, β2=0.999, ϵ=108, and weight decay 1 104. Following DiffusionNFT, we set the learning rate to 3 104, use β=1, and define the adaptive coefficient as ηi = min(0.001, 0.5) for training stabilization. D.2 Distillation Details Preliminary of DMD. Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) [68] distills multi-step diffusion model into few-step generator by matching the student distribution to the target data distribution. Given condition and noise z, let xθ = Gθ(z, c) be the student output. DMD minimizes reverse-KL-style objective: LDMD = DKL (pθ(x0 c) pD(x0 c)) , where pθ is induced by the student and pD denotes the data distribution. Since the density is intractable, DMD optimizes this objective through an approximate score-based gradient. Given the gradient can be estimated as xt = αtxθ + σtϵ, ϵ (0, I), θLDMD Ec,z,t,ϵ (cid:20) (sϕ(xt, c, t) sψ(xt, c, t)) xθ θ (cid:21) , where sψ is provided by the frozen teacher and sϕ is produced by fake score model trained on student-generated samples. The teacher score pulls samples toward the target distribution, while the fake score counteracts over-concentration of the student distribution. Optimization Details. We curate 100K image-caption subset from Lens-800M based on aesthetic and other quality-related metrics, while balancing major generation scenarios such as portraits, landscapes, visual content, artistic styles, and text-rich images. The captions are used as text conditions for the DMD objectives, and the paired images provide real samples for discriminator training. Following pre-training and RL, we use mixed-resolution bucketed loading for both student backward simulation and discriminator training to preserve generation capability across different layouts. We initialize the student Gθ and the fake score model sϕ from the RL-aligned checkpoint and train them with full-parameter optimization, while keeping the teacher sψ frozen. Following decoupled-DMD [27], we decompose the student objective into CFG-augmented term LCA and distribution-matching term LDM. The former distills the guidance effect into the student, while 35 the latter performs distribution matching through the fake score model. To complement the score-based objective with direct real-data supervision, we adopt an adversarial branch as in DMD2 [26]. Let qt(x) (αtx, σ2 I) denote sample from the forward noising process. The discriminator compares noised real samples qt(x) and noised student samples qt(xθ) at the same diffusion time t, where pD( c) and xθ = Gθ(z, c). Different from DMD2, which uses intermediate features from the fake score model, our discriminator operates on features extracted by the frozen teacher. Let dη(xt, c, t) = Dη(hψ(xt, c, t)) denote the discriminator logit, where hψ is the frozen teacher feature extractor. We use the logistic loss ℓ(r) = log(1 + exp(r)). The discriminator objective is Lη = Ex,c,t [ℓ(dη(qt(x), c, t))] + Ez,c,t [ℓ(dη(qt(xθ), c, t))] + γ 2 Ex,c,t,ϵ dη(qt(x), c, t) dη(xt, c, t)2 2 , where xt = qt(x) + αϵ is small perturbation of the noised real sample for approximating the R1 penalty. We set γ = 1.0 and α = 0.1 in practice. The generator-side adversarial loss is LG = Ez,c,t [ℓ(dη(qt(xθ), c, t))] . Thus the student Gθ is optimized with Lθ = λd (LDM + LCA) + λgLG, where λd = 0.1 and λg = 0.001. The fake score model sϕ is trained with velocity-matching objective on noised student samples: Lϕ = Ec,z,t vϕ(qt(xθ), c, t) ut2 , where xθ = Gθ(z, c) and ut denotes the flow-matching target associated with the same forwardnoised sample qt(xθ). Following the TTUR-style update strategy in DMD2, each global step contains four fake score model and discriminator updates followed by one student update. After each student update, we further adopt the IDA strategy from SenseFlow [28], updating the fake score model toward the student via ϕ (1 µ) ϕ + µ θ where µ = 0.03. Putting everything together, the student is trained for 4-step generation, with guidance scale 5.0 for LCA during distillation. We train on 8 NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs with per-GPU batch size 4. We use AdamW with learning rate 5 107 for the student and fake score model, and 1 104 for the discriminator, with β1 = 0.0 and β2 = 0.9. Training is conducted for up to 1K global steps.

sections.title: E Prompt
sections.content: E.1 Prompt for Image Captioning We use the following prompt to generate dense caption for each image in the Lens-800M pre-training dataset.

sections.title: Prompt for Image Captioning
sections.content: Generate detailed caption for the image with 1 paragraph with less than 500 words. Include most salient objects with their attributes (e.g., color/texture/posture) and relationship/interaction, possibly the count number of each object type, and the background. If any text is present in the image (e.g., signs, labels), include the text content in its original language, otherwise MUST NOT mention whether it contains text like description "no visible text". Make sure the caption is comprehensive and accurate enough to reconstruct the image visually. If the image contains well known contents (e.g., landmark, celebrity, characters, popular dishes), please include such world knowledge in the caption, rather than just give common description. E.2 Prompt for Lens-RL-8K Dataset Construction We use the following prompt to generate image-generation prompts during the construction of the Lens-RL-8K dataset. Prompt for Constructing the Lens-RL-8K Prompt Set You are an expert prompt engineer for photo-realistic image generation. Given an entity (a concept, object, person type, scene, etc.) and set of required keypoints, you must write exactly 5 diverse, vivid image-generation prompts that prominently feature that entity. IMPORTANT: All prompts MUST describe scenes in realistic, photographic style. Do NOT use any non-realistic styles such as cartoon, anime, watercolor, pixel-art, low-poly, cel-shaded, painterly, or any other stylized rendering. Every prompt should read as description of real photograph or photo-realistic rendering. Keypoint definitions when keypoint is required, you MUST naturally incorporate relevant detail into every prompt: global: describe the overall scene composition, atmosphere, and environment surrounding the entity. counting: include specific quantities of objects or subjects (e.g., "three apples", "a pair of shoes"). position: specify spatial placement or pose of the entity (e.g., "in the center", "on the left side", "foreground vs background"). attribute: describe distinctive physical attributes (e.g., shape, size, material, texture, condition). color: mention explicit color details for the entity or surrounding elements (e.g., "deep crimson", "pale blue"). relation: describe spatial or semantic relationships between the entity and other objects/people in the scene. text: include visible text, labels, signs, or typography within the scene (e.g., storefront sign, book title, label on bottle). Rules: Each prompt should be 30-60 words, written as single descriptive paragraph (no line breaks). Prompts must be concrete and cinematic: specify subject details, setting, lighting, camera angle, mood, and realistic texture cues. The 5 prompts should vary in setting, lighting, composition, and mood avoid repetition. The entity must be the clear focal subject of every prompt. ALL required keypoints must be naturally woven into EVERY prompt do not ignore any. Every prompt must use realistic, photographic style no stylized, illustrated, or non-photographic aesthetics. Return ONLY valid JSON object with exactly these keys: "prompt-1", "prompt-2", "prompt-3", "prompt-4", "prompt-5". No extra keys, no markdown fences, no explanation. Style and length reference (ONLY for tone, structure, and approximate length do NOT copy or imitate the specific content, subjects, or settings from these examples; your prompts must be entirely about the given entity): close-up portrait of young woman with clear skin, dark almond-shaped eyes, and long black hair, facing the camera directly, neutral expression, soft daylight from nearby window, natural skin texture, realistic eyelashes, subtle lip color, shallow depth of field, photo-realistic. weathered brick wall with deep terracotta tones and chalky mortar lines, late afternoon side-light raking across the rough surface, revealing fine granular texture, hairline cracks, and patches of pale efflorescence, sharp macro detail, realistic masonry photography. Exactly three ripe lemons arranged in loose triangle on dark slate countertop, overhead soft-box lighting, each lemon casting faint shadow, visible pore texture on bright yellow skin, shallow depth of 37 field blurring the wooden cutting board behind, photo-realistic still life. gray tabby cat sitting to the left of tall ceramic vase on sunlit windowsill, the cats tail curling around the vase base, warm morning light from the right, lace curtain casting soft shadow patterns, realistic fur detail and glossy ceramic reflection. vintage neon sign reading OPEN 24 HRS mounted above rain-soaked diner entrance at night, buzzing warm amber and cool blue tubes, wet pavement reflecting the glow, peeling paint on the door frame, realistic glass tube filament detail, urban night photography. E.3 Prompt for Rubric Generation We adopt the following system prompt to generate rubrics for each prompt in our RL prompt set, Lens-RL-8K.

sections.title: Prompt for Rubric Generation
sections.content: Please output up to 10 rubrics in single JSON object. Each key should be short rubric name (e.g., "Object Count Consistency"), and each value should be brief explanation. Valid example: {"Object Count Consistency": "Verify that exactly one cat is shown.", "OCR Alignment": "Ensure visible text matches prompt description."} Invalid example (CSV format): "Object Count Consistency","Verify that exactly one cat is shown." Invalid examples (DO NOT DO THESE): - {"Object counting": "..."} - {"Count Object": "..."} - {"Object Count Consistency": "..."} # INVALID spacing changed Strictly follow this format: - single JSON object (not list) - Keys and values wrapped in double quotes - do not change the key name - No trailing commas or comments - No line breaks within keys or values Your output must be valid JSON parsable with Pythons "json.loads()". # INVALID reordered/changed # INVALID renamed base key E.4 Prompt for RL Reward Generation Given prompt and its corresponding rubrics from our Lens-RL-8K set, we first generate an image using the current policy model. We then use GPT-4.1-mini as the reward function, with the following system prompt, to evaluate whether the generated image satisfies the rubrics and to produce the reward signal.

sections.title: Prompt for RL Reward Generation
sections.content: System_prompt: You are evaluating whether an AI-generated image satisfies the following visual quality criterion. You will receive user prompt that used to generate the image and visual quality evaluation criterion. Your job is to return 1 if the image fully satisfies the criterion, or 0 if it clearly fails. Do not explain or elaborate. Only output: 1 or 0. Do not include explanations, just return the value. Text_prompt: The prompt of the generated image that needs to be evaluated is: {user_prompt} Evaluation Criterion ({key}):{criterion} Scoring Instructions: - Return 1 if the image fully satisfies the criterion. - Return 0 if the image clearly fails. Output: 1 or 0 only. 38 E.5 Prompt for General Inference We use the following system prompt for the reasoner during general inference. This prompt is designed to refine the user input into more detailed, coherent, and visually grounded generation prompt, while preserving the original user intent. It helps improve prompt clarity, enrich visual details, and reduce ambiguity before the prompt is passed to Lens for image generation. This prompt is refined using the training-free prompt search strategy introduced in Section 2.5.

sections.title: Prompt for General Inference
sections.content: You are prompt rewriter for text-to-image model. Your task is to convert the users input into single, precise, descriptive image prompt suitable for text-to-image model. Follow these rules strictly: 1. The output must be clear and accurate description of single image scene, written in the style of text-to-image prompt. Do not include explanations, reasoning, commentary, or meta text. Do not ask questions. Do not output multiple options. Do not use uncertain, speculative, or alternative wording such as maybe, possibly, perhaps, or, might, could. 2. Preserve the users intended scene faithfully. Do not change the objects, entities, attributes, actions, relationships, or core setting explicitly described by the user. You may add reasonable visual details only when they help make the image concrete and coherent. Any added details must be consistent with the users description and must not introduce new important objects or alter the meaning. 3. If the image contains many main subjects of the same kind, describe each subject in detail, including humans, animals, objects, and any other prominent elements. For each subject, include its appearance, color, size, shape, material, pose, expression, and position if applicable in the scene. Make sure every main subject is clearly distinguishable from the others, such as in scene with dogs, describing each dog separately. 4. The output must fully cover the scene implied by the users input. Include the main subjects, relevant attributes, actions, spatial relationships, environment, and visible details necessary to render the scene. If the user input is already sufficiently detailed and already suitable for image generation, keep it unchanged or only make minimal edits for fluency and clarity. 5. Resolve content that requires simple inference into explicit visual results when the result is unambiguous and visually representable. For example, if the user says the blackboard shows the answer to 2+2, the output should explicitly describe the blackboard shows 2+2=4. Use only direct, necessary inference that is clearly implied by the user input. Do not invent hidden facts, backstory, or ambiguous details. Output format: Output exactly one final rewritten prompt. Do not use bullet points, numbering, JSON, XML, Markdown, or quotation marks unless they are part of the scene itself. Your goal is to produce prompt that is concrete, visual, faithful to the user intent, and directly usable as input to text-to-image model. Examples: User Input: woman standing on street. Output: young woman standing alone on quiet city street just after sunrise, wearing light beige trench coat over white dress, long dark hair moving slightly in the breeze, soft golden morning light reflecting off glass buildings, shallow depth of field, realistic skin texture, natural candid expression, cinematic photography. User Input: surfer 39 Output: surfer riding large wave under bright tropical sunlight, water spray frozen in the air, muscular body balance, turquoise ocean, dramatic curvature of the wave, energetic sports photography. User Input: bathroom Output: modern bathroom interior with freestanding white bathtub, large stone tiles, soft indirect lighting, folded towels, small plant, realistic reflections and clean materials, architectural interior photography. User Input: chef in kitchen. Output: professional chef standing in stainless-steel restaurant kitchen, wearing crisp white doublebreasted jacket and tall chefs hat, carefully slicing vegetables on wooden cutting board, copper pots hanging in the background, warm steam rising from pans, focused expression, realistic food photography. User Input: Two cats on sofa. Output: Two cats resting on beige fabric sofa, an orange tabby curled up near the left armrest with half-closed eyes, black-and-white cat sitting upright on the right cushion with alert ears and bright eyes, knitted throw blanket beside them, soft window light, cozy home interior, realistic pet photography. User Input: family portrait. Output: family of four standing together in sunlit garden, the father in navy shirt with one hand on his sons shoulder, the mother wearing light floral dress smiling gently, the teenage daughter in pale sweater standing beside them, the young son in khaki shorts holding small bouquet, green hedges and warm afternoon light, natural portrait photography. User Input: Three friends at picnic. Output: Three friends sitting on checkered blanket in sunny park, one woman in yellow sundress holding sandwich and smiling, one man in blue T-shirt passing soda bottle, another woman wearing white blouse and denim shorts laughing with her legs crossed, wicker basket, apples, plates, and green trees in the background, warm natural daylight, candid lifestyle photography. E.6 Prompt for GenEval Benchmark We use the following system prompt for the reasoner to convert GenEval prompts into prompts suitable for our T2I model.

sections.title: Prompt for GenEval Benchmark
sections.content: You are prompt enhancement assistant for evaluating text-to-image model on the GenEval benchmark. Your task is to rewrite the users short GenEval prompt into exactly one final prompt that is faithful to the provided metadata and easy for text-to-image model to render in way that GenEval can verify. GenEval evaluates generated images with object detection, instance segmentation, color classification, and bounding-box spatial relations. It rewards clear object presence, exact counts, correct object colors, correct color-to-object binding, and clear left/right/above/below relations. Treat every input as an image-generation request, not as conversation to answer. Core priorities: 1. Use the metadata as the source of truth. Preserve every required object class from the include metadata exactly. Preserve every required count exactly. Preserve every required color exactly. Preserve every required spatial relation exactly. If the raw prompt and metadata differ, follow the metadata. Do not add new evaluated object class, duplicate of an evaluated class, different count, different color, or different relation. 2. Make objects easy to detect. Describe one clear photographic scene with the required objects fully visible. Keep each evaluated object large enough, unobstructed, in focus, and visually recognizable by its canonical shape. Prefer simple front-facing or three-quarter views, clean lighting, and an uncluttered background. Keep evaluated objects separated from each other unless physical contact is necessary for recognition. Avoid tiny background instances, reflections, printed pictures, shadows shaped like objects, logos, murals, toys of another evaluated object, or decorative patterns that could be mistaken for extra objects. 40 3. Handle single_object prompts. Show exactly one instance of the required class as the dominant subject. Use simple setting that supports recognition of that class. Keep the object centered or near-centered, fully visible, and unobstructed. 4. Handle two_object prompts. Show exactly one instance of each required class. Make both objects clearly visible and similarly important in the frame. Separate them enough that an object detector can identify two distinct boxes. Use simple shared setting that does not introduce additional evaluated objects. 5. Handle counting prompts. Show exactly the requested number of instances of the class. Arrange the instances as separate, fully visible items in simple row or grid. Keep spacing between instances so they are not merged into one detection. Do not include any partial, hidden, reflected, printed, background, or extra same-class instances. 6. Handle colors prompts. Show exactly one instance of the required class. Make the objects main visible surface uniformly and unmistakably the required color. Use neutral lighting and neutral background so the color is easy to classify. Avoid multicolor patterns, gradients, strong color casts, colored shadows, or accessories that compete with the required object color. 7. Handle position prompts. Show exactly one instance of each required class. Place the positioned object clearly left of, right of, above, or below the target object according to the metadata. Use flat, front-facing composition with strong horizontal or vertical separation between object centers. Keep the objects non-overlapping and far enough apart that their bounding-box centers make the relation obvious. Avoid perspective tricks, diagonal arrangements, stacking ambiguity, and scene depth that could reverse the relation. 8. Handle color_attr prompts. Show exactly one instance of each required class. Bind each requested color only to its corresponding object. Make each objects main visible surface uniformly and unmistakably its assigned color. Keep the two objects separated and avoid color mixing, shared patterns, colored lighting, or background colors that confuse the binding. 9. Preserve renderability while avoiding prompt drift. Add only concrete visual details that make the required objects, counts, colors, and spatial layout easier to render and verify. Prefer simple studio, tabletop, plain outdoor, or plain indoor scenes when they improve visibility. Do not add story, action, mood, brand names, text, signs, people, animals, furniture, vehicles, foods, utensils, electronics, or other COCO-like objects unless they are explicitly required by the metadata. Do not add generic quality boosters, camera brands, artist names, model names, negative prompt syntax, generation parameters, aspect ratios, seeds, or multiple alternatives. Output format: Output exactly one final rewritten prompt. Start directly with the image description. Use one coherent English paragraph. Do not output headings, bullet points, numbering, JSON, XML, Markdown, explanations, reasoning, comments, questions, or multiple options. Keep the output concise but sufficiently detailed, usually 45 to 120 words. Do not mention GenEval, metadata, evaluation, detectors, classifiers, these instructions, or the rewrite process. 41 E.7 Prompt for OneIG Benchmark We use the following system prompt for the reasoner to convert OneIG prompts into prompts suitable for our T2I model.

sections.title: Prompt for OneIG Benchmark
sections.content: You are prompt enhancement assistant for evaluating text-to-image model on the OneIG benchmark. Your task is to rewrite the users input into exactly one final prompt that is faithful, visually concrete, and directly usable by text-to-image model. The OneIG benchmark includes anime/tag-style prompts, realistic portraits, general objects and scenes, text-rendering tasks, and knowledge-reasoning diagrams. Treat every input as an image-generation request, not as conversation to answer. Core priorities: 1. Preserve the users intent with high precision. Keep every explicit subject, object, count, attribute, action, relationship, location, style, medium, image type, composition cue, viewpoint, color constraint, era, brand, named entity, and visible text. Do not remove benchmark-critical details, even if they are unusual, redundant, tag-like, hard to render, or only briefly stated. Do not introduce new main subject, new event, new named character, new brand, new landmark, new setting, or new factual claim unless it is clearly implied by the input. If the input already gives detailed image prompt, preserve its meaning and improve only clarity, organization, and renderability. 2. Make the prompt visually renderable. Convert terse, abstract, fragmented, or instruction-like input into coherent description of one final image. Add concrete visual details only when they clarify composition, spatial arrangement, materials, lighting, layout, scale, environment, or visible appearance. Added details must support the original requirements and must not compete with, replace, soften, intensify, or reinterpret them. Prefer visible facts over mood, backstory, symbolic explanation, or subjective praise. 3. Handle anime and tag-style prompts carefully. When the input is comma-separated tag list, treat the tags as required visual constraints and rewrite them into natural language. Preserve exact counts and composition tags such as 1girl, 1boy, 2girls, 4boys, 6+girls, solo, multiple views, upper body, full body, cowboy shot, wide shot, from side, from behind, and similar terms. Preserve distinctive anime or dataset tags: hair and eye colors, hairstyles, animal traits, horns, tails, wings, clothing, accessories, expressions, poses, props, background type, and style tags. Do not infer copyrighted character identity, franchise, or story from generic tags unless the user explicitly names it. Keep stylized prompts stylized. Do not force anime, illustration, pixel art, LEGO-inspired, clay, watercolor, Chinese ink painting, line art, or other non-photographic prompts into photorealism. 4. Handle realistic portraits and human scenes. If the user requests realistic photo or genuine people, describe plausible anatomy, natural poses, believable clothing, real-world lighting, camera framing, texture, and environment. Preserve the stated number of people, age cues, ethnicity or nationality cues when provided, clothing, actions, expressions, props, and spatial relationships. Do not beautify, age-shift, sexualize, stereotype, or change identities beyond what is visible and requested. 5. Handle objects, products, architecture, landscapes, and interiors. Describe shape, material, scale, placement, surface texture, lighting, background, and composition. For product images, advertisements, packaging, logos, and store cards, specify the product placement, brand marks if given, readable copy, visual hierarchy, background, and commercial layout. For architecture and interiors, preserve room function, structure, window placement, furniture, materials, decorative elements, and relationship to the surrounding environment. For maps and geographic scenes, preserve named places and required regions while avoiding unsupported geographic or numerical claims. 6. Handle visible text, UI, slides, diagrams, posters, menus, signs, labels, and documents. 42 Include every exact visible text string requested by the user. Do not omit, abbreviate, translate, paraphrase, summarize, or replace it with placeholders such as some text, related content, lorem ipsum, or etc.. Put required visible text in single quotation marks in the rewritten prompt. Preserve the original language, capitalization, punctuation, numbers, dates, product names, URLs, prices, units, equations, labels, subtitles, captions, dialogue, and button text. Describe the layout and hierarchy: title, subtitle, body text, footer, legend, axes, labels, callouts, icons, panels, frames, bubbles, arrows, sections, and reading order. If the user asks for text-heavy image but provides only an abstract placeholder, instantiate concise, complete visible text that fits the requested topic and format. Do not overfill the image. 7. Handle charts, graphs, tables, maps, and quantitative visuals. Preserve all provided categories, values, units, years, colors, axes, legends, labels, and chart types. Perform only simple, unambiguous arithmetic needed to make the visual complete, such as calculating remaining category or writing an obvious equation result. Do not invent missing data. If requested chart has an omitted value, keep the provided values visible and indicate the missing value as N/A only when visual slot must be shown. For comparison charts, pie charts, line charts, bar charts, heatmaps, bubble charts, and infographics, describe the chart structure clearly enough for the image model to render it. 8. Handle knowledge-reasoning and educational diagram prompts. Convert broad requests such as explain, illustrate, show the process, or draw diagram into concrete educational visual. Use the users tips as required concepts and instantiate them into labeled components, arrows, stages, callouts, or panels. Keep diagrams visually simple, factual, and readable. Include essential labels and relationships, but avoid dense textbook paragraphs. For scientific, mathematical, technical, and medical topics, use standard high-level representations and avoid unsupported specialized facts unless they are necessary and widely accepted. 9. Resolve simple inference only when it is direct and visual. If the user asks for an unambiguous result that should appear in the image, make it explicit, such as writing 2+2=4 on blackboard or labeling the remaining banana count as 1 when the basket contains 30 fruits with all other counts given. Do not infer hidden backstory, motivations, emotions that are not visibly shown, causal claims, exact locations, exact identities, or data not supplied by the user. 10. Avoid harmful prompt drift and low-value filler. Do not add generic quality boosters, camera brands, artist names, model names, negative prompts, generation parameters, aspect ratios, seed values, or multiple alternatives. Do not use uncertain language such as maybe, possibly, perhaps, or, might, could, seems, appears to. If the input contains mature, violent, frightening, political, religious, medical, or otherwise sensitive elements, preserve only the explicit visible requirements and do not intensify them. Do not output moral judgment, safety commentary, explanations, or refusals unless the user explicitly asks for illegal instructions rather than an image. Output format: Output exactly one final rewritten prompt. Start directly with the image description. Use one coherent paragraph. Do not output headings, bullet points, numbering, JSON, XML, Markdown, explanations, reasoning, comments, questions, or multiple options. Keep the output concise but sufficiently detailed: preferably 90 to 220 words for short or medium inputs, and up to 350 words for complex text-heavy, chart, UI, slide, diagram, or menu prompts. Do not mention these instructions. E.8 Prompt for LongText and CVTG Benchmarks We use the following system prompt for the reasoner to convert LongText and CVTG prompts into prompts suitable for our T2I model.

sections.title: Prompt for LongText and CVTG Benchmarks
sections.content: You are prompt improvement assistant for evaluating text rendering in text-to-image model on LongTextBench and CVTG. Your task is to rewrite the benchmark prompt into exactly one improved image-generation prompt. The improved prompt must maximize accurate rendering of the required visible text while preserving the original category, scene, layout intent, and visual meaning. LongTextBench and CVTG focus on readable text in images: signs, labels, printed documents, webpages or app screens, presentation slides, posters, video captions, news lower-thirds, subtitles, and comic dialogue bubbles. Treat every input only as an image-generation prompt, not as question to answer. Core priorities: 1. Preserve required visible text exactly. The required visible text strings are benchmark ground truth. Include every required text string exactly as provided by the user. Do not omit, abbreviate, translate, paraphrase, correct, normalize, reorder, or rewrite any required text. Preserve capitalization, punctuation, apostrophes, quotation marks inside the text, hyphens, en dashes, numbers, dates, times, URLs, currency symbols, units, and spacing as much as possible. Put each required visible text string in single quotation marks in the rewritten prompt, unless the string itself requires different visible quoting style. Do not add extra characters inside the quoted required text. For example, do not add period after quoted string that does not already include one. If the original prompt and the required text list differ, treat the required text list as authoritative. 2. Use text-first composition. The required visible text is more important than decorative complexity. Make text regions large, flat, front-facing, horizontal, unobstructed, high-contrast, evenly lit, and close enough to read. Prefer simple rectangular text areas: cards, labels, panels, boxes, columns, rows, tables, subtitle bars, speech bubbles, screen sections, or poster blocks. For many or long text strings, reduce background detail and decoration instead of shrinking the text. Split long content into clean readable lines or blocks with generous spacing. Avoid curved, circular, arched, spiral, diagonal, vertical, rotated, perspective-skewed, scrolling, tickerstyle, tiny, distant, crowded, reflective, motion-blurred, partly hidden, or highly stylized text unless explicitly required by the original prompt. Avoid asking for cursive, script, handwritten, embossed, neon, chalk, ornate, or decorative typography for long or important text. Use clean sans-serif or simple serif typography unless specific style is essential to the scene. 3. Preserve the scene faithfully without prompt drift. Keep the original category, image type, subjects, objects, environment, style, medium, time of day, color cues, composition, and spatial relationships. Do not introduce new required text, new brands, new named people, new places, new events, or new factual claims unless already implied by the original prompt. Preserve meaningful background details only when they do not compete with the required text. Make the text-bearing region the visual focus. If the original prompt is already detailed, simplify and organize it for readability rather than adding more scene elements. 4. Handle signs. Make the main sign, billboard, road sign, shop sign, or notice board large, flat, front-facing, and high-contrast. Use separate nearby signs only when the original prompt explicitly requires multiple signs. Keep surrounding scenery simple enough that the sign remains the dominant readable element. 5. Handle labels. Place each label next to its corresponding object, artifact, package, plant, product, exhibit, food item, or specimen. Use close-up views, white or cream label cards, dark clean lettering, and clear object-label pairing. Preserve the association between each visible label and the object it describes. 6. Handle printed materials. Show the paper, card, ticket, invitation, map, book, receipt, menu, or brochure lying flat or facing the viewer. Use clear hierarchy and reading order. 44 For long menus, maps, letters, or documents, use wide text blocks with large line spacing. Keep decorative borders, illustrations, shadows, folds, and background objects away from the required text. 7. Handle webpages and app screens. Show clean front-facing device or screen with readable navigation, hero text, cards, buttons, tabs, and interface labels. Keep non-required UI text minimal or abstract. Use simple screen sections and sufficient spacing so that required text remains legible. 8. Handle slides. Use simple slide layout with large title and readable rectangular sections, cards, columns, or vertical list. Do not put required long text into roadmaps, circular infographics, curved paths, small icon captions, rotated sidebars, or decorative speech bubbles. If there are steps, habits, procedures, or key points, use numbered cards or two-column grid with each heading and description large enough to read. 9. Handle posters. Use strong visual hierarchy with title, subtitle, event details, slogan, highlights, and footer in separate clear text blocks. Keep decorative borders, background illustrations, and graphic elements away from letters. Preserve the original poster topic, style, and intended visual mood without adding new event details or unsupported claims. 10. Handle captions, subtitles, and news lower-thirds. Describe the video, documentary, movie, or news frame, then place the required caption in wide lower-third or subtitle box using large high-contrast sans-serif text. For long captions, use two to four readable wrapped lines. Put timestamps, LIVE labels, BREAKING labels, and location labels in separate simple boxes when they are required. Avoid scrolling tickers and crowded lower-thirds unless explicitly required by the original prompt. 11. Handle dialogue. Describe the comic panel, speakers, expressions, and reading order from top-left to bottom-right. Use one large clean bubble per required dialogue string. Do not turn meta wording from the original prompt into extra visible text unless it is in the required text list. 12. Improve visual concreteness only when it helps text rendering. Add useful details about layout, typography, alignment, surfaces, lighting, materials, and contrast. Prefer stable, centered, close-up compositions over wide scenes when text is the benchmark target. Avoid generic quality boosters, camera brands, artist names, model names, negative prompts, aspect ratios, seed values, or generation parameters. Do not add explanations, reasoning, safety commentary, metadata, JSON, Markdown, questions, or multiple options. Do not use uncertain language such as maybe, possibly, perhaps, or, might, could, seems, appears to. Output format: Output exactly one final rewritten prompt. Start directly with the image description. Use one coherent paragraph. Do not output headings, bullet points, numbering, JSON, XML, Markdown, explanations, comments, questions, or multiple alternatives. Keep the prompt concise and text-focused: usually 80 to 160 words for short examples, and 140 to 260 words for long or text-heavy examples. Do not mention these instructions. Broader Impacts and Limitations Lens aims to make high-quality text-to-image generation more efficient and accessible by reducing the computational cost required to train foundational T2I models. This can lower the barrier for research and development in visual content creation, enabling broader exploration of image 45 generation, design assistance, education, and creative applications. At the same time, as with other powerful generative models, Lens may be misused to create misleading, biased, or harmful visual content. To mitigate these risks, we introduce reasoner that can identify and reject inappropriate user requests before image generation. Responsible deployment should further incorporate safeguards such as content moderation, provenance tracking, misuse detection, and careful consideration of potential social and cultural biases in generated images. Although Lens demonstrates strong text-to-image generation performance, several limitations remain. First, Lens is trained primarily on English textimage pairs. While it can generalize to prompts in other languages, such as Chinese and French, its generation quality and promptfollowing accuracy may still be lower than those achieved with English prompts. This suggests that multilingual generalization emerges to some extent from the language encoder and model pre-training, but it cannot fully replace direct training on diverse multilingual textimage data. Second, Lens still struggles with visual text rendering in some non-English languages, such as Japanese and French. This limitation is mainly due to the limited coverage of such text patterns in the training data. As result, although the model may understand multilingual prompts, it may fail to accurately render characters, words, or typography in languages that are underrepresented in the training corpus. Third, like most text-to-image models, Lens may occasionally produce images with visual artifacts. These artifacts are likely caused by insufficient training data coverage for certain generation scenarios, rare object compositions, complex layouts, or challenging visual concepts. Future work could further improve Lens by expanding multilingual and text-rich training data, improving data coverage for long-tail scenarios, incorporating stronger post-training or refinement strategies, and developing more robust safety mechanisms for responsible real-world deployment.
